## THEORETICAL ANALYSIS OF SELF-TRAINING WITH DEEP NETWORKS ON UNLABELED DATA

## **Colin Wei & Kendrick Shen & Yining Chen & Tengyu Ma**

Department of Computer Science Stanford University Stanford, CA 94305, USA {colinwei,kshen6,cynnjjs,tengyuma}@stanford.edu

## ABSTRACT

Self-training algorithms, which train a model to fit pseudolabels predicted by another previously-learned model, have been very successful for learning with unlabeled data using neural networks. However, the current theoretical understanding of self-training only applies to linear models. This work provides a unified theoretical analysis of self-training with deep networks for semi-supervised learning, unsupervised domain adaptation, and unsupervised learning. At the core of our analysis is a simple but realistic “expansion” assumption, which states that a lowprobability subset of the data must expand to a neighborhood with large probability relative to the subset. We also assume that neighborhoods of examples in different classes have minimal overlap. We prove that under these assumptions, the minimizers of population objectives based on self-training and input-consistency regularization will achieve high accuracy with respect to ground-truth labels. By using off-the-shelf generalization bounds, we immediately convert this result to sample complexity guarantees for neural nets that are polynomial in the margin and Lipschitzness. Our results help explain the empirical successes of recently proposed self-training algorithms which use input consistency regularization.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Though supervised learning with neural networks has become standard and reliable, it still often requires massive _labeled_ datasets. As labels can be expensive or difficult to obtain, leveraging unlabeled data in deep learning has become an active research area. Recent works in semi-supervised learning (Chapelle et al., 2010; Kingma et al., 2014; Kipf & Welling, 2016; Laine & Aila, 2016; Sohn et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020) and unsupervised domain adaptation (Ben-David et al., 2010; Ganin & Lempitsky, 2015; Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Hoffman et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019) leverage lots of unlabeled data as well as labeled data from the same distribution or a related distribution. Recent progress in unsupervised learning or representation learning (Hinton et al., 1999; Doersch et al., 2015; Gidaris et al., 2018; Misra & Maaten, 2020; Chen et al., 2020a;b; Grill et al., 2020) learns high-quality representations without using any labels.

Self-training is a common algorithmic paradigm for leveraging unlabeled data with deep networks. Self-training methods train a model to fit pseudolabels, that is, predictions on unlabeled data made by a previously-learned model (Yarowsky, 1995; Grandvalet & Bengio, 2005; Lee, 2013). Recent work also extends these methods to enforce stability of predictions under input transformations such as adversarial perturbations (Miyato et al., 2018) and data augmentation (Xie et al., 2019). These approaches, known as input consistency regularization, have been successful in semi-supervised learning (Sohn et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020), unsupervised domain adaptation (French et al., 2017; Shu et al., 2018), and unsupervised learning (Hu et al., 2017; Grill et al., 2020).

Despite the empirical successes, theoretical progress in understanding how to use unlabeled data has lagged. Whereas supervised learning is relatively well-understood, statistical tools for reasoning about unlabeled data are not as readily available. Around 25 years ago, Vapnik (1995) proposed the transductive SVM for unlabeled data, which can be viewed as an early version of self-training, yet there is little work showing that this method improves sample complexity (Derbeko et al., 2004).

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Working with unlabeled data requires proper assumptions on the input distribution (Ben-David et al., 2008). Recent papers (Carmon et al., 2019; Raghunathan et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020c; Kumar et al., 2020; Oymak & Gulcu, 2020) analyze self-training in various settings, but mainly for linear models and often require that the data is Gaussian or near-Gaussian. Kumar et al. (2020) also analyze self-training in a setting where gradual domain shift occurs over multiple timesteps but assume a small Wasserstein distance bound on the shift between consecutive timesteps. Another line of work leverages unlabeled data using non-parametric methods, requiring unlabeled sample complexity that is _exponential_ in dimension (Rigollet, 2007; Singh et al., 2009; Urner & Ben-David, 2013).

This paper provides a unified theoretical analysis of self-training _with deep networks_ for semisupervised learning, unsupervised domain adaptation, and unsupervised learning. Under a simple and realistic expansion assumption on the data distribution, we show that self-training with input consistency regularization using a deep network can achieve high accuracy on true labels, using unlabeled sample size that is polynomial in the margin and Lipschitzness of the model. Our analysis provides theoretical intuition for recent empirically successful self-training algorithms which rely on input consistency regularization (Berthelot et al., 2019; Sohn et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020).

Our expansion assumption intuitively states that the data distribution has good continuity within each class. Concretely, letting _Pi_ be the distribution of data conditioned on class _i_ , expansion states that for small subset _S_ of examples with class _i_ ,

where and _c >_ 1 is the expansion factor. The neighborhood will be defined to incorporate data augmentation, but for now can be thought of as a collection of points with a small _ℓ_ 2 distance to _S_ . This notion is an extension of the Cheeger constant (or isoperimetric or expansion constant) (Cheeger, 1969) which has been studied extensively in graph theory (Chung & Graham, 1997), combinatorial optimization (Mohar & Poljak, 1993; Raghavendra & Steurer, 2010), sampling (Kannan et al., 1995; Lovász & Vempala, 2007; Zhang et al., 2017), and even in early versions of self-training (Balcan et al., 2005) for the co-training setting (Blum & Mitchell, 1998). Expansion says that the manifold of each class has sufficient connectivity, as every subset _S_ has a neighborhood larger than _S_ . We give examples of distributions satisfying expansion in Section 3.1. We also require a separation condition stating that there are few neighboring pairs from different classes.

Our algorithms leverage expansion by using input consistency regularization (Miyato et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2019) to encourage predictions of a classifier _G_ to be consistent on neighboring examples:

For unsupervised domain adaptation and semi-supervised learning, we analyze an algorithm which fits _G_ to pseudolabels on unlabeled data while regularizing input consistency. Assuming expansion and separation, we prove that the fitted model will denoise the pseudolabels and achieve high accuracy on the true labels (Theorem 4.3). This explains the empirical phenomenon that self-training on pseudolabels often improves over the pseudolabeler, despite no access to true labels.

For unsupervised learning, we consider finding a classifier _G_ that minimizes the input consistency regularizer with the constraint that enough examples are assigned each label. In Theorem 3.6, we show that assuming expansion and separation, the learned classifier will have high accuracy in predicting true classes, up to a permutation of the labels (which can’t be recovered without true labels).

The main intuition of the theorems is as follows: input consistency regularization ensures that the model is locally consistent, and the expansion property magnifies the local consistency to global consistency within the same class. In the unsupervised domain adaptation setting, as shown in Figure 1 (right), the incorrectly pseudolabeled examples (the red area) are gradually denoised by their correctly pseudolabeled neighbors (the green area), whose probability mass is non-trivial (at least _c −_ 1 times the mass of the mistaken set by expansion). We note that expansion is only required on the _population_ distribution, but self-training is performed on the empirical samples. Due to the extrapolation power of parametric methods, the local-to-global consistency effect of expansion occurs _implicitly_ on the population. In contrast, nearest-neighbor methods would require expansion to occur _explicitly_ on empirical samples, suffering the curse of dimensionality as a result. We provide more details below, and visualize this effect in Figure 1 (left).
To our best knowledge, this paper gives the first analysis with polynomial sample complexity guarantees for deep neural net models for unsupervised learning, semi-supervised learning, and unsuper-

Prior works (Rigollet, 2007; Singh et al., 2009; Urner & Ben-David, 2013) analyzed nonparametric methods that essentially recover the data distribution exactly with unlabeled data, but require sample complexity exponential in dimension. Our approach optimizes parametric loss functions and regularizers, so guarantees involving the population loss can be converted to finite sample results using off-the-shelf generalization bounds (Theorem 3.7). When a neural net can separate ground-truth classes with large margin, the sample complexities from these bounds can be small, that is, polynomial in dimension.

Finally, we note that our regularizer _R_ ( _·_ ) corresponds to enforcing consistency w.r.t. adversarial examples, which was shown to be empirically helpful for semi-supervised learning (Miyato et al., 2018; Qiao et al., 2018) and unsupervised domain adaptation (Shu et al., 2018). Moreover, we can extend the notion of neighborhood in (1.1) to include data augmentations of examples, which will increase the neighborhood size and therefore improve the expansion. Thus, our theory can help explain empirical observations that consistency regularization based on aggressive data augmentation or adversarial training can improve performance with unlabeled data (Shu et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2019; Berthelot et al., 2019; Sohn et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020a).

In summary, our contributions include: 1) we propose a simple and realistic expansion assumption which states that the data distribution has connectivity within the manifold of a ground-truth class 2) using this expansion assumption, we provide ground-truth accuracy guarantees for self-training algorithms which regularize input consistency on unlabeled data, and 3) our analysis is easily applicable to deep networks with polynomial unlabeled samples via off-the-shelf generalization bounds.

## 1.1 ADDITIONAL RELATED WORK

Self-training via pseudolabeling (Lee, 2013) or min-entropy objectives (Grandvalet & Bengio, 2005) has been widely used in both semi-supervised learning (Laine & Aila, 2016; Tarvainen & Valpola, 2017; Iscen et al., 2019; Yalniz et al., 2019; Berthelot et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020; Sohn et al., 2020) and unsupervised domain adaptation (Long et al., 2013; French et al., 2017; Saito et al., 2017; Shu et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2019). Our paper studies input consistency regularization, which enforces stability of the prediction w.r.t transformations of the unlabeled data. In practice, these transformations include adversarial perturbations, which was proposed as the VAT objective (Miyato et al., 2018), as well as data augmentations (Xie et al., 2019).

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

For unsupervised learning, our self-training objective is closely related to BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), a recent state-of-the-art method which trains a student model to match the representations predicted by a teacher model on strongly augmented versions of the input. Contrastive learning is another popular method for unsupervised representation learning which encourages representations of “positive pairs”, ideally consisting of examples from the same class, to be close, while pushing negative pairs far apart (Mikolov et al., 2013; Oord et al., 2018; Arora et al., 2019). Recent works in contrastive learning achieve state-of-the-art representation quality by using strong data augmentation to form positive pairs (Chen et al., 2020a;b). The role of data augmentation here is in spirit similar to our use of input consistency regularization. Less related to our setting are algorithms which learn representations by solving self-supervised pretext tasks, such as inpainting and predicting rotations (Pathak et al., 2016; Noroozi & Favaro, 2016; Gidaris et al., 2018). Lee et al. (2020) theoretically analyze self-supervised learning, but their analysis applies to a different class of algorithms than ours.

Prior theoretical works analyze contrastive learning by assuming access to document data distributed according to a particular topic modeling setup (Tosh et al., 2020) or pairs of independent samples within the same class (Arora et al., 2019). However, the assumptions required for these analyses do not necessarily apply to vision, where positive pairs apply different data augmentations to the same image, and are therefore strongly correlated. Other papers analyze information-theoretic properties of representation learning (Tian et al., 2020; Tsai et al., 2020).

Prior works analyze continuity or “cluster” assumptions for semi-supervised learning which are related to our notion of expansion (Seeger, 2000; Rigollet, 2007; Singh et al., 2009; Urner & BenDavid, 2013). However, these papers leverage unlabeled data using non-parametric methods, requiring unlabeled sample complexity that is exponential in the dimension. On the other hand, our analysis is for parametric methods, and therefore the unlabeled sample complexity can be low when a neural net can separate the ground-truth classes with large margin.

Co-training is a classical version of self-training which requires two distinct “views” (i.e., feature subsets) of the data, each of which can be used to predict the true label on its own (Blum & Mitchell, 1998; Dasgupta et al., 2002; Balcan et al., 2005). For example, to predict the topic of a webpage, one view could be the incoming links and another view could be the words in the page. The original co-training algorithms (Blum & Mitchell, 1998; Dasgupta et al., 2002) assume that the two views are independent conditioned on the true label and leverage this independence to obtain accurate pseudolabels for the unlabeled data. By contrast, if we cast our setting into the co-training framework by treating an example and a randomly sampled neighbor as the two views of the data, the two views are highly correlated. Balcan et al. (2005) relax the requirement on independent views of co-training, also by using an “expansion” assumption. Our assumption is closely related to theirs and conceptually equivalent if we cast our setting into the co-training framework by treating neighboring examples are two views. However, their analysis requires confident pseudolabels to all be accurate and does not rigorously account for potential propagation of errors from their algorithm. In contrast, our contribution is to propose and analyze an objective function involving input consistency regularization whose minimizer _denoises_ errors from potentially incorrect pseudolabels. We also provide finite sample complexity bounds for the neural network hypothesis class and analyze unsupervised learning algorithms.

Alternative theoretical analyses of unsupervised domain adaptation assume bounded measures of discrepancy between source and target domains (Ben-David et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2019). Balcan & Blum (2010) propose a PAC-style framework for analyzing semi-supervised learning, but their bounds require the user to specify a notion of compatability which incorporates prior knowledge about the data, and do not apply to domain adaptation. Globerson et al. (2017) demonstrate semisupervised learning can outperform supervised learning in labeled sample complexity but assume full knowledge of the unlabeled distribution. (Mobahi et al., 2020) show that for kernel methods, self-distillation, a variant of self-training, can effectively amplify regularization. Their analysis is for kernel methods, whereas our analysis applies to deep networks under data assumptions.

## 3 EXPANSION PROPERTY AND GUARANTEES FOR UNSUPERVISED LEARNING

In this section we will first introduce our key assumption on expansion. We then study the implications of expansion for unsupervised learning. We show that if a classifier is consistent w.r.t. input transformations and predicts each class with decent probability, the learned labels will align with ground-truth classes up to permutation of the class indices (Theorem 3.6).

## 3.1 EXPANSION PROPERTY

We introduce the notion of expansion. As our theory studies objectives which enforce stability to input transformations, we will first model allowable transformations of the input _x_ by the set _B_ ( _x_ ), defined below. We can think of _r_ as a value much smaller than the typical norm of _x_ , so the probability _P_ ( _B_ ( _x_ )) is exponentially small in dimension. Our theory easily applies to other choices of _B_ , though we set this definition as default for simplicity. Now we define the neighborhood of _x_ , denoted by _N_ ( _x_ ), as the set of points whose transformation sets overlap with that of _x_ :

We note that this definition considers the _population_ distribution, and expansion is not expected to hold on the training set, because all empirical examples are far away from each other, and thus the neighborhoods of training examples do not overlap. The notion is closely related to the _Cheeger constant_ , which is used to bound mixing times and hitting times for sampling from continuous distributions (Lovász & Vempala, 2007; Zhang et al., 2017), and _small-set expansion_ , which quantifies connectivity of graphs (Hoory et al., 2006; Raghavendra & Steurer, 2010). In particular, when the neighborhood is defined to be the collection of points with _ℓ_ 2 distance at most _r_ from the set, then the expansion factor _c_ is bounded below by exp( _ηr_ ), where _η_ is the Cheeger constant (Zhang et al., 2017). In Section E.1, we use GANs to demonstrate that expansion is a realistic property in vision. For unsupervised learning, we require expansion with _a_ = 1 _/_ 2 and _c >_ 1:

## 3.2 POPULATION GUARANTEES FOR UNSUPERVISED LEARNING

We design an unsupervised learning objective which leverages the expansion and separation properties. Our objective is on the population distribution, but it is parametric, so we can extend it to the finite sample case in Section 3.3. We wish to learn a classifier _G_ : _X →_ [ _K_ ] using only unlabeled data, such that predicted classes align with ground-truth classes. Note that without observing any labels, we can only learn ground-truth classes up to permutation, leading to the following permutation-invariant error defined for a classifier _G_ :
We study the following unsupervised population objective over classifiers _G_ : _X →_ [ _K_ ], which encourages input consistency while ensuring that predicted classes have sufficient probability.

Here _c_ is the expansion coefficient in Assumption 3.2. The constraint ensures that the probability of any predicted class is larger than the input consistency loss. Let _ρ_ ≜ min _y∈_ [ _K_ ] _P_ ( _{x_ : _G[⋆]_ ( _x_ ) = _y}_ ) denote the probability of the smallest ground-truth class. The following theorem shows that when _P_ satisfies expansion and separation, the global minimizer of the objective (3.4) will have low error.

In Section C, we provide the proof of Theorem 3.6 as well as a variant of the theorem which holds for a weaker additive notion of expansion. By applying the generalization bounds of Section 3.3, we can convert Theorem 3.6 into a finite-sample guarantees that are polynomial in margin and Lipschitzness of the model (see Theorem D.1).

Our objective is reminiscent of recent methods which achieve state-of-the-art results in unsupervised representation learning: SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020a), MoCov2 (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b), and BYOL (Grill et al., 2020). Unlike our algorithm, these methods do not predict discrete labels, but rather, directly predict a representation which is consistent under input transformations, However, our analysis still suggests an explanation for why input consistency regularization is so vital for these methods: assuming the data satisfies expansion, it encourages representations to be similar over the _entire_ class, so the representations will capture ground-truth class structure.

Chen et al. (2020a) also observe that using more aggressive data augmentation for regularizing input stability results in significant improvements in representation quality. We remark that our theory offers a potential explanation: in our framework, strengthening augmentation increases the size of the neighborhood, resulting in a larger expansion factor _c_ and improving the accuracy bound (3.5).

## 3.3 FINITE SAMPLE GUARANTEES FOR DEEP LEARNING MODELS

In this section, we show that if the ground-truth classes are separable by a neural net with large robust margin, then generalization can be good. The main advantage of Theorem 3.6 and Theorem 4.3 over prior work is that they analyze parametric objectives, so finite sample guarantees immediately hold via off-the-shelf generalization bounds. Prior work on continuity or “cluster” assumptions related to expansion require nonparametric techniques with a sample complexity that is exponential in dimension (Seeger, 2000; Rigollet, 2007; Singh et al., 2009; Urner & Ben-David, 2013).

We apply the generalization bound of (Wei & Ma, 2019b) based on a notion of all-layer margin, though any other bound would work. The all-layer margin measures the stability of the neural net to simultaneous perturbations to each hidden layer. Formally, suppose that _G_ ( _x_ ) ≜ arg max _i F_ ( _x_ ) _i_ is the prediction of some feedforward neural network _F_ : _X →_ R _[K]_ which computes the following function: _F_ ( _x_ ) = _Wpφ_ ( _· · · φ_ ( _W_ 1 _x_ ) _· · ·_ ) with weight matrices _{Wi}[p] i_ =1[. ## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we propose an expansion assumption on the data which allows for a unified theoretical analysis of self-training for semi-supervised and unsupervised learning. Our assumption is realistic for real-world datasets, particularly in vision. Our analysis is applicable to deep neural networks and can explain why algorithms based on self-training and input consistency regularization can perform so well on unlabeled data. We hope that this assumption can facilitate future theoretical analyses and inspire theoretically-principled algorithms for semi-supervised and unsupervised learning. For example, an interesting question for future work is to extend our assumptions to analyze domain adaptation algorithms based on aligning the source and target (Hoffman et al., 2018).

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is an exemplary piece of theoretical machine learning research, characterized by its impressive ability to bridge the gap between empirical deep learning success and rigorous mathematical foundations. The writing is clear, logical, and provides a compelling narrative that motivates the need for a unified theoretical framework. Its most significant strength lies in the introduction of the "expansion" assumption—a geometrically intuitive condition that successfully generalizes previous, overly restrictive assumptions (such as Gaussianity) and provides a tractable mechanism for explaining how local consistency regularization leads to global label propagation. By translating these geometric properties into polynomial sample complexity bounds for deep neural networks, the authors provide a foundational result that is both theoretically meaningful and contextually relevant to modern practitioners. The primary challenge for the authors, then, is to ensure that the后续 (subsequent) technical sections maintain this level of clarity, specifically in how the "neighborhood" definitions in the expansion assumption interact with the complexities of deep network architectures in high-dimensional spaces without becoming overly abstract.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding self-training with deep networks under an expansion-based assumption, and it is commendable for its ambition to unify analyses across semi-supervised learning, domain adaptation, and unsupervised learning. The writing is generally clear, with a well-motivated introduction and a careful exposition of the core assumptions. However, there are several significant issues that limit the paper’s impact. First, while the expansion and separation assumptions are elegantly stated, their verifiability and realism for high-dimensional, real-world data remain questionable—the examples given in Section 3.1 are likely too simplistic to justify the claim that the assumptions are “realistic.” Second, the analysis is confined to population objectives, and the sample complexity guarantees rely on off-the-shelf generalization bounds that are polynomial in the margin and Lipschitzness; the practical implications of these bounds are left unexamined, and the Lipschitz constraints may be difficult to enforce or even approximate with standard neural networks. Third, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of how the expansion factor and neighborhood definition interact with the choice of data augmentation, as the current treatment is somewhat abstract. Finally, while the theoretical intuition is insightful, the gap between the assumptions made and the actual empirical settings where self-training succeeds (e.g., with complex augmentations and adversarial perturbations) is not adequately bridged, which weakens the claimed explanatory power for recent algorithms.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding self-training with deep networks under an expansion-based assumption, and it is commendable for its ambition to unify analyses across semi-supervised learning, domain adaptation, and unsupervised learning. The writing is generally clear, with a well-motivated introduction and a careful exposition of the core assumptions. However, there are several significant issues that limit the paper’s impact. First, while the expansion and separation assumptions are elegantly stated, their verifiability and realism for high-dimensional, real-world data remain questionable—the examples given in Section 3.1 are likely too simplistic to justify the claim that the assumptions are “realistic.” Second, the analysis is confined to population objectives, and the sample complexity guarantees rely on off-the-shelf generalization bounds that are polynomial in the margin and Lipschitzness; the practical implications of these bounds are left unexamined, and the Lipschitz constraints may be difficult to enforce or even approximate with standard neural networks. Third, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of how the expansion factor and neighborhood definition interact with the choice of data augmentation, as the current treatment is somewhat abstract. Finally, while the theoretical intuition is insightful, the gap between the assumptions made and the actual empirical settings where self-training succeeds (e.g., with complex augmentations and adversarial perturbations) is not adequately bridged, which weakens the claimed explanatory power for recent algorithms.

The context from earlier prose (e.g., 'a low-probability subset of the data must expand to a neighborhood with large probability relative to the subset') suggests the intended form is Pi(N(V)) ≥ c Pi(V), but the actual mathematical condition is absent. comments.title: Unjustified claim that neighbors of mistaken pseudolabels are correctly pseudolabeled
comments.quote: the incorrectly pseudolabeled examples (the red area) are gradually denoised by their correctly pseudolabeled neighbors (the green area), whose probability mass is non-trivial (at least _c −_ 1 times the mass of the mistaken set by expansion).
comments.explanation: The text asserts that the green area consists of "correctly pseudolabeled neighbors" and that its mass is at least (c-1) times the mass of the red area. Expansion gives Pr[N(S)] ≥ c·Pr[S] for the mistaken set S, so Pr[N(S) \ S] ≥ (c-1)·Pr[S]. This lower bound applies to the entire neighborhood of S, not only to the subset of neighbors that are correctly pseudolabeled. Without additional assumptions (e.g., that the pseudolabeler is accurate on the vast majority of the class, so that the vast majority of the neighborhood is correctly labeled), we cannot conclude that the correctly pseudolabeled portion of the neighborhood inherits this lower bound. The separation assumption prevents neighborhood overlap between classes, but does not guarantee that the pseudolabels on those neighbors are correct. The formal proof in the paper likely uses a more careful argument that combines expansion with a bound on the total error of the pseudolabeler; however, the prose as written over-simplifies this step and could give readers the mistaken impression that expansion alone ensures that neighbors of a mistaken set are mostly correct. This is a minor issue in an introductory passage, but it warrants flagging because the reasoning as stated is incomplete.

If L_{0-1} measured agreement, the sign would be plausible (minus encourages agreement), but that contradicts the earlier definition and the term 'loss for fitting pseudolabels'. The combination of this sign and the ambiguous L_{0-1} definition makes the objective ambiguous and likely erroneous.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a timely and well-motivated contribution to the theoretical understanding of self-training in deep learning, effectively addressing a notable gap in the literature that has historically been limited to linear models or restrictive distributional assumptions. The introduction is exceptionally clear, logically structured, and does a strong job positioning the work at the intersection of semi-supervised learning, consistency regularization, and geometric data assumptions. The proposed “expansion” assumption is intuitively grounded and offers a promising mechanism for lifting local consistency to global generalization guarantees. However, the most significant concern lies in the practical relevance and interpretability of the theoretical framework: the expansion and separation conditions, while mathematically tractable, are difficult to verify or empirically justify for the high-dimensional, manifold-structured distributions typical of modern deep learning, and their realism remains largely unexamined. Furthermore, the sample complexity bounds, though polynomial in the margin and Lipschitz constant, may be loose in practice since these quantities are notoriously unstable, poorly optimized, and highly sensitive to architectural choices in neural networks. The analysis also currently operates at the population level, overlooking critical finite-sample and optimization realities of self-training, such as pseudo-label corruption, iterative noise accumulation, and non-convex optimization dynamics. To elevate the paper, the authors should provide more concrete justification or empirical evidence for the realism of their assumptions, derive tighter or more architecture-aware bounds, and explicitly discuss how the guarantees degrade under practical optimization and sampling constraints.

comments.title: Inconsistency in characterizing polynomial sample complexity dependence
comments.quote: When a neural net can separate ground-truth classes with large margin, the sample complexities from these bounds can be small, that is, polynomial in dimension.
comments.explanation: I was initially concerned by the statement that the sample complexity is 'polynomial in dimension.' I checked the abstract and introduction provided in the context, where the authors explicitly emphasize that their method requires 'unlabeled sample size that is polynomial in the margin and Lipschitzness of the model.' Here, however, they shift the focus to dimension alone. While many margin-based generalization bounds do scale with dimension, the deliberate omission of margin/Lipschitz terms here creates a slight inconsistency with their own summary. In high-dimensional settings, complexity scaling polynomially with dimension alone (without explicit margin dependence) can still be problematic unless the dimension refers to an intrinsic/covering dimension or the constants absorb it. The authors likely intend to convey that the dimension-dependent terms are manageable given large margins, but the current phrasing oversimplifies and contradicts their earlier emphasis on margin/Lipschitz dependence. Clarifying this would align the body text with their theoretical contributions and avoid misleading readers about the primary drivers of polynomial sample complexity.

comments.title: Missing formula for neighborhood definition

Now we define the neighborhood of x , denoted by N(x) , as the set of points whose transformation sets overlap with that of x :

For S ⊆X , we define the neighborhood of S as the union of neighborhoods of its elements: _N_ ( _S_ ) ≜ _∪x∈SN_ ( _x_ ).
comments.explanation: The paragraph introduces the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$ verbally but cuts off before providing the formal mathematical definition, likely due to a dropped displayed equation during PDF extraction. The definition should logically be $\mathcal{N}(x) \triangleq \{x' \in \mathcal{X} : \mathcal{B}(x') \cap \mathcal{B}(x) \neq \emptyset\}$. comments.title: Typo in disjoint support condition
comments.quote: We assume that each example _x_ has a unique label, so _Pi_ , _Pj_ have disjoint support for _i_ = _j_ .
comments.explanation: The text claims that class-conditional distributions $P_i$ and $P_j$ have disjoint support for $i = j$. This is mathematically nonsensical, as a distribution cannot have disjoint support with itself. The authors clearly intend to state that the supports are disjoint for $i \neq j$, which is standard for unique-label classification settings. This is a minor typographical error that contradicts the intended mathematical meaning.

comments.title: Contradiction between ball radius and parameter r in Example 3.4
comments.quote: Suppose the transformation set B(x) is an ℓ_2-ball with radius 2√d around x, so there is no data augmentation and r = 1
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the simultaneous statement that $B(x)$ has radius $2\sqrt{d}$ while explicitly setting $r=1$. Checking Definition 3.1, the neighborhood set is formally defined as $B(x) \triangleq \{x' : \exists T \in \mathcal{T} \text{ s.t. } \|x' - T(x)\| \le r\}$. When there is no data augmentation ($\mathcal{T}=\{I\}$), $B(x)$ reduces to an $\ell_2$-ball of radius exactly $r$. Therefore, claiming a radius of $2\sqrt{d}$ while setting $r=1$ directly contradicts the paper's own definition. Furthermore, the subsequent separation condition requires inter-class mean distances to be $\gtrsim \sqrt{\log d}$, which aligns with concentration bounds for a small radius ($r \sim 1$) but is inconsistent with a large radius ($2\sqrt{d}$). The authors likely intended $r = 2\sqrt{d}$ or mistakenly labeled the radius; this mismatch obscures the true scaling assumptions of the example.

comments.title: Garbled probability constraint in Theorem 3.6
comments.explanation: The condition on the minimum class probability $\rho$ in Theorem 3.6 is severely corrupted by OCR artifacts. Based on standard expansion lemmas and the context, this likely intends to state a bound such as $\rho > \max\{\frac{c-1}{c-2}, 2\}\mu$ or a similar rational function of $c$. As currently rendered, the inequality is mathematically unreadable. While likely a typesetting error, it must be corrected before publication to ensure the theorem's prerequisites are precisely stated and verifiable.

comments.title: Unclear covariance scaling in Example 3.4
comments.quote: Pi ≜ N(τi, d[1] _[I][d][×][d]_[) with isotropic covariance
comments.explanation: The covariance matrix in the Gaussian mixture definition is mangled (`d[1] _[I]`). Given the high-dimensional setting and the subsequent reliance on concentration inequalities for separation and expansion proofs, the exact scaling of the covariance (e.g., $I$, $\frac{1}{d}I$, or $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}I$) is critical. The current notation obscures the variance parameter $\sigma^2$, making it impossible to verify the claimed distance bounds or the validity of the example's expansion/separation claims. I suggest explicitly stating the intended covariance scaling to allow readers to reproduce the example's analysis.

comments.title: Inverted margin property and thresholding inequality
comments.quote: For now, we simply note that m has the property that if G (x) = y, then m (F, x, y) = 0, so we can upper bound the 0-1 loss by thresholding the all-layer margin: **1** ( G (x) = y) _≤_ **1** ( m (F, x, y) _≥ t_ ) for any t > 0.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly aim to leverage the all-layer margin to derive generalization bounds, a standard and rigorous approach in margin-based learning theory. However, the stated property (that the margin is exactly 0 when the prediction is correct) directly contradicts the provided thresholding inequality. If G(x)=y, the left-hand side of the indicator inequality is 1, but the right-hand side evaluates to 1(0 ≥ t), which is 0 for any t > 0, yielding the false statement 1 ≤ 0. In standard margin definitions (including the cited Wei & Ma, 2019b), a positive margin indicates correct classification, and the correct 0-1 loss bound typically takes the form 1(G(x) ≠ y) ≤ 1(m(F,x,y) < t). The text appears to have inverted both the sign convention for the margin at correct predictions and the direction of the indicator inequality. This mathematical inconsistency breaks the stated link between margin stability and the subsequent generalization theorem.

comments.title: Contradiction between 'disagreement' label and equality indicator definition
comments.quote: For G, G[′] : _X →_ [ _K_ ], define _L_ 0-1( _G, G[′]_ ) ≜ E _P_ [ **1** ( _G_ ( _x_ ) = _G[′]_ ( _x_ ))] to be the disagreement between _G_ and _G[′] _.
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition uses an equality indicator 1(G(x) = G'(x)), which computes the probability of *agreement* between two classifiers. However, the prose explicitly identifies this quantity as 'disagreement'. In the context of self-training and pseudolabeling, the algorithm's goal is to fit the pseudolabeler, meaning the objective should minimize disagreement (i.e., maximize agreement). If the defined quantity is actually agreement, minimizing it would encourage the model to actively disagree with the pseudolabels, contradicting the stated algorithmic paradigm and making Lemma 4.2 (which bounds error by this objective) logically incoherent. The definition almost certainly contains a typo and should use 1(G(x) ≠ G'(x)). Without this correction, the theoretical objective is inverted.

comments.title: Severe OCR corruption obscures key parameter comparison
comments.quote: First, Section 3.2 requires expansion withon all sets with probability less thanprobability less than _a_ ¯, which 1can _/_ 2, whereas Assumptionbe much smaller than 4.11 _/_ only requires expansion on sets2.
comments.explanation: Reading this sentence, I initially struggled to parse the mathematical comparison being made due to heavy text corruption. Checking against the paper's established assumptions, the authors are clearly contrasting Assumption 3.2 (which requires $(1/2, c)$-expansion, valid for sets up to mass $1/2$) with Assumption 4.1 (which requires $(\bar{a}, \bar{c})$-expansion only for smaller sets corresponding to pseudolabel errors, with $\bar{a} < 1/3$). The authors correctly identify that relaxing the expansion requirement to apply only to smaller sets is a core reason the error bounds differ. However, the current garbled text renders the specific thresholds unreadable and could mislead a careful reader unfamiliar with the earlier definitions. Restoring it to something like '...requires expansion on all sets with probability less than $a = 1/2$, whereas Assumption 4.1 only requires expansion on sets with probability much smaller than $1/2$.' would resolve the ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding Lemma 4.2 and consistency loss
comments.quote: On the other hand, Lemma 4.2 gives an error bound for _all_ classifiers, regardless of _RB_ ( _G_ ).
comments.explanation: I was initially confused by the phrase 'regardless of $R_B(G)$.' Lemma 4.2 bounds the classifier error by the full objective $L(G)$, which explicitly contains the input consistency loss $R_B(G)$ as a term. While the inequality $\text{Err}(G) \le L(G)$ mathematically holds for any classifier $G$ (even those with arbitrarily large consistency loss), stating it applies 'regardless of $R_B(G)$' might incorrectly imply that the consistency loss does not influence or appear in the bound. In reality, a larger $R_B(G)$ directly scales/relaxes the upper bound on the error. The authors rightly point out that unlike Section 3.2 (which focuses on minimizers of the consistency objective with low $R_B(G)$), Lemma 4.2 is a universal structural bound that does not restrict the classifier's consistency value. However, the phrasing should clarify that $R_B(G)$ is an explicit component of the bounding quantity, rather than being irrelevant to the bound itself.

For S ⊆X , we define the neighborhood of S as the union of neighborhoods of its elements: _N_ ( _S_ ) ≜ _∪x∈SN_ ( _x_ ).
comments.explanation: The paragraph introduces the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$ verbally but cuts off before providing the formal mathematical definition, likely due to a dropped displayed equation during PDF extraction. The definition should logically be $\mathcal{N}(x) \triangleq \{x' \in \mathcal{X} : \mathcal{B}(x') \cap \mathcal{B}(x) \neq \emptyset\}$. Omitting this leaves the notation $\mathcal{N}(x)$ formally undefined before it is immediately used to define $\mathcal{N}(S)$.

comments.title: Typo in disjoint support condition
comments.quote: We assume that each example _x_ has a unique label, so _Pi_ , _Pj_ have disjoint support for _i_ = _j_ .
comments.explanation: The text claims that class-conditional distributions $P_i$ and $P_j$ have disjoint support for $i = j$. This is mathematically nonsensical, as a distribution cannot have disjoint support with itself. The authors clearly intend to state that the supports are disjoint for $i \neq j$, which is standard for unique-label classification settings. This is a minor typographical error that contradicts the intended mathematical meaning.

comments.title: Contradiction between ball radius and parameter r in Example 3.4
comments.quote: Suppose the transformation set B(x) is an ℓ_2-ball with radius 2√d around x, so there is no data augmentation and r = 1
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the simultaneous statement that $B(x)$ has radius $2\sqrt{d}$ while explicitly setting $r=1$. Checking Definition 3.1, the neighborhood set is formally defined as $B(x) \triangleq \{x' : \exists T \in \mathcal{T} \text{ s.t. } \|x' - T(x)\| \le r\}$. When there is no data augmentation ($\mathcal{T}=\{I\}$), $B(x)$ reduces to an $\ell_2$-ball of radius exactly $r$. Therefore, claiming a radius of $2\sqrt{d}$ while setting $r=1$ directly contradicts the paper's own definition. Furthermore, the subsequent separation condition requires inter-class mean distances to be $\gtrsim \sqrt{\log d}$, which aligns with concentration bounds for a small radius ($r \sim 1$) but is inconsistent with a large radius ($2\sqrt{d}$). The authors likely intended $r = 2\sqrt{d}$ or mistakenly labeled the radius; this mismatch obscures the true scaling assumptions of the example.

comments.quote:
expansion states that for small subset S of examples with class i,

where and c > 1 is the expansion factor.
comments.explanation: The prose describing the expansion assumption in Section 1 appears to have a missing equation between "class i," and "where and c > 1...". While this is almost certainly a PDF-to-OCR extraction artifact where a displayed equation was dropped during formatting, it leaves a core theoretical assumption undefined in the main text. A careful reader would need to skip ahead to Definition 3.1 to understand the condition. I recommend explicitly restating the expansion inequality (likely $\Pi_i(S) \le a \implies \Pi_i(\mathcal{N}(S)) \ge c \cdot \Pi_i(S)$) in the introduction to maintain self-containment and clarity.

comments.title: Garbled probability constraint in Theorem 3.6
comments.explanation: The condition on the minimum class probability $\rho$ in Theorem 3.6 is severely corrupted by OCR artifacts. Based on standard expansion lemmas and the context, this likely intends to state a bound such as $\rho > \max\{\frac{c-1}{c-2}, 2\}\mu$ or a similar rational function of $c$. As currently rendered, the inequality is mathematically unreadable. While likely a typesetting error, it must be corrected before publication to ensure the theorem's prerequisites are precisely stated and verifiable.

comments.title: Unclear covariance scaling in Example 3.4
comments.quote: Pi ≜ N(τi, d[1] _[I][d][×][d]_[) with isotropic covariance
comments.explanation: The covariance matrix in the Gaussian mixture definition is mangled (`d[1] _[I]`). Given the high-dimensional setting and the subsequent reliance on concentration inequalities for separation and expansion proofs, the exact scaling of the covariance (e.g., $I$, $\frac{1}{d}I$, or $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}I$) is critical. The current notation obscures the variance parameter $\sigma^2$, making it impossible to verify the claimed distance bounds or the validity of the example's expansion/separation claims. I suggest explicitly stating the intended covariance scaling to allow readers to reproduce the example's analysis.

comments.title: Inverted margin property and thresholding inequality
comments.quote: For now, we simply note that m has the property that if G (x) = y, then m (F, x, y) = 0, so we can upper bound the 0-1 loss by thresholding the all-layer margin: **1** ( G (x) = y) _≤_ **1** ( m (F, x, y) _≥ t_ ) for any t > 0.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly aim to leverage the all-layer margin to derive generalization bounds, a standard and rigorous approach in margin-based learning theory. However, the stated property (that the margin is exactly 0 when the prediction is correct) directly contradicts the provided thresholding inequality. If G(x)=y, the left-hand side of the indicator inequality is 1, but the right-hand side evaluates to 1(0 ≥ t), which is 0 for any t > 0, yielding the false statement 1 ≤ 0. In standard margin definitions (including the cited Wei & Ma, 2019b), a positive margin indicates correct classification, and the correct 0-1 loss bound typically takes the form 1(G(x) ≠ y) ≤ 1(m(F,x,y) < t). The text appears to have inverted both the sign convention for the margin at correct predictions and the direction of the indicator inequality. This mathematical inconsistency breaks the stated link between margin stability and the subsequent generalization theorem.

comments.title: Contradiction between 'disagreement' label and equality indicator definition
comments.quote: For G, G[′] : _X →_ [ _K_ ], define _L_ 0-1( _G, G[′]_ ) ≜ E _P_ [ **1** ( _G_ ( _x_ ) = _G[′]_ ( _x_ ))] to be the disagreement between _G_ and _G[′] _.
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition uses an equality indicator 1(G(x) = G'(x)), which computes the probability of *agreement* between two classifiers. However, the prose explicitly identifies this quantity as 'disagreement'. In the context of self-training and pseudolabeling, the algorithm's goal is to fit the pseudolabeler, meaning the objective should minimize disagreement (i.e., maximize agreement). If the defined quantity is actually agreement, minimizing it would encourage the model to actively disagree with the pseudolabels, contradicting the stated algorithmic paradigm and making Lemma 4.2 (which bounds error by this objective) logically incoherent. The definition almost certainly contains a typo and should use 1(G(x) ≠ G'(x)). Without this correction, the theoretical objective is inverted.

comments.title: Severe OCR corruption obscures key parameter comparison
comments.quote: First, Section 3.2 requires expansion withon all sets with probability less thanprobability less than _a_ ¯, which 1can _/_ 2, whereas Assumptionbe much smaller than 4.11 _/_ only requires expansion on sets2.
comments.explanation: Reading this sentence, I initially struggled to parse the mathematical comparison being made due to heavy text corruption. Checking against the paper's established assumptions, the authors are clearly contrasting Assumption 3.2 (which requires $(1/2, c)$-expansion, valid for sets up to mass $1/2$) with Assumption 4.1 (which requires $(\bar{a}, \bar{c})$-expansion only for smaller sets corresponding to pseudolabel errors, with $\bar{a} < 1/3$). The authors correctly identify that relaxing the expansion requirement to apply only to smaller sets is a core reason the error bounds differ. However, the current garbled text renders the specific thresholds unreadable and could mislead a careful reader unfamiliar with the earlier definitions. Restoring it to something like '...requires expansion on all sets with probability less than $a = 1/2$, whereas Assumption 4.1 only requires expansion on sets with probability much smaller than $1/2$.' would resolve the ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding Lemma 4.2 and consistency loss
comments.quote: On the other hand, Lemma 4.2 gives an error bound for _all_ classifiers, regardless of _RB_ ( _G_ ).
comments.explanation: I was initially confused by the phrase 'regardless of $R_B(G)$.' Lemma 4.2 bounds the classifier error by the full objective $L(G)$, which explicitly contains the input consistency loss $R_B(G)$ as a term. While the inequality $\text{Err}(G) \le L(G)$ mathematically holds for any classifier $G$ (even those with arbitrarily large consistency loss), stating it applies 'regardless of $R_B(G)$' might incorrectly imply that the consistency loss does not influence or appear in the bound. In reality, a larger $R_B(G)$ directly scales/relaxes the upper bound on the error. The authors rightly point out that unlike Section 3.2 (which focuses on minimizers of the consistency objective with low $R_B(G)$), Lemma 4.2 is a universal structural bound that does not restrict the classifier's consistency value. However, the phrasing should clarify that $R_B(G)$ is an explicit component of the bounding quantity, rather than being irrelevant to the bound itself.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous and significant theoretical contribution that effectively fills a major gap in the literature regarding self-training with deep neural networks for semi-supervised and unsupervised learning. The exposition is clear and high-quality, particularly in its introduction of the novel "Expansion" assumption, which serves as a mathematically elegant and intuitive generalization of the Cheeger constant to justify the use of input consistency regularization. However, the most significant issue regarding the analysis is the practical applicability of this core assumption. While theoretically sound, the "Expansion" assumption strictly requires that the data distribution exhibits strong manifold connectivity and continuity within each class, which may not hold for highly noisy, uniform, or heavily disjoint distributions, potentially limiting the theoretical robustness of the derived sample complexity guarantees for real-world datasets.

comments.title: Logical Contradiction: Assumption 4.1 is 'Stronger' than Claimed
comments.quote: First, Section 3.2 requires expansion on all sets with probability less than $a$, whereas Assumption 4.1 only requires expansion on sets with probability less than $1/2$. Second, ... This discrepancy is due to weaker requirements on the expansion
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state that the requirements in Assumption 4.1 (which applies to sets with probability less than $1/2$ and requires an expansion factor $c>3$) are 'weaker' than Section 3.2 (which applies to sets with probability less than $1/2$ and requires $c>1$). However, in standard expansion theory (related to Cheeger constants), the expansion factor required is typically *higher* for smaller sets. Specifically, Assumption 4.1 applies to a smaller mass ($ ar{a} < 1/3$ vs $1/2$) and requires a significantly larger expansion factor ($c>3$ vs $c>1$). A requirement on a smaller, high-density set to expand by a factor of 3 is mathematically a much stricter or 'harder' condition, not a weaker one. The authors seem to confuse the 'scope' of the assumption (applying to a single error set vs all sets) with the 'difficulty' of satisfying the assumption. This claim undermines the logic of why Theorem 4.3 has a higher error bound than Theorem 3.6.

comments.title: Phrasing: 'Ablation Studies for Pseudolabeling'
comments.quote: In Section E.2, we perform ablation studies for pseudolabeling which show that components of our theoretical objective (4.1) do improve performance.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'ablation studies for pseudolabeling' suggests that the subject of the study is the pseudolabeler algorithm itself (i.e., varying the pseudolabeler). However, the context suggests the authors are abating components of the *proposed self-training objective* (specifically, the mix of consistency regularization and pseudolabeling). A standard phrasing would be 'ablation studies *on* self-training' or 'ablation studies *of* the theoretical objective'. This wording could mislead a reader into thinking the paper analyzes pseudolabeling in isolation, rather than the proposed unified framework.
